ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for garlic roti; closest parent 'flatbread food product' (FOODON_03302051) used as the ontology anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC (Flat bread) is the closest defensible parent; no specific garlic roti or tandoor roti code exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 226559009 'Flatbread (food)' used as closest parent; no specific garlic roti concept exists in SNOMED CT.

serving.natural_language_portion: One garlic roti piece typically weighs approximately 90 g, representing a single round flatbread as served in an Indian restaurant context.

nutrient_source.title: Roti (Chapati) with fat, cooked — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017 revised edition), adapted for garlic and butter additions
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Base values derived from ICMR-NIN 'Roti (Chapati) with fat' entry (per 100 g cooked). Garlic contributes negligible macronutrient change at typical topping quantities (~5 g per roti). Butter finish (~3 g per roti) increases fat and energy slightly; values adjusted upward by approximately 3 g fat and 27 kcal per 100 g relative to plain roti with fat to reflect the butter application described in the restaurant preparation. USDA FoodData Central (SR Legacy, NDB 20081) consulted as secondary cross-check for whole wheat flatbread macros.

gi_source.title: Chapati, wheat flour, India — Glycemic Index Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 62 derived from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for wheat flour chapati / roti (India), which reports a GI range of 52–62 for whole wheat flatbreads cooked in a tandoor or on a tawa. The upper value of 62 is applied here to account for the butter finish, which does not substantially alter GI but the garlic topping is nutritionally negligible for GI purposes. GL per serving calculated as: (62 × 43.7 g available carbohydrate per 90 g serving) / 100 = 27.1, rounded to 27.0.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~310 mg per 100 g) and butter addition
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to one roti and request minimal butter, as the combined sodium from dough and butter can contribute meaningfully to daily sodium targets of 2000 mg; garlic itself may offer a modest vasodilatory benefit.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium glycemic index (GI 62) and moderate carbohydrate load (~43.7 g available carbohydrate per serving)
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should treat garlic roti as a moderate-GI carbohydrate choice; pairing one roti with a high-fibre dal or vegetable curry can blunt the postprandial glucose response, and portion control to one piece per meal is advisable.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from butter (~4.1 g per 100 g) and overall energy density
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For individuals managing cardiovascular disease, the butter applied during serving adds saturated fat; requesting a dry or minimal-butter version and limiting intake to one roti per meal is recommended, while the whole wheat base provides beneficial dietary fibre (~3.8 g per 100 g).

llm_training.natural_language_description: Garlic Roti is an Indian whole wheat flatbread topped with freshly chopped garlic and cooked in a traditional clay tandoor oven at high heat, then finished with a knob of butter at the time of serving. The tandoor imparts a characteristic smoky char and slight crispness to the outer surface while keeping the interior soft and slightly chewy. The garlic caramelises lightly against the hot bread surface, delivering a pungent, savoury aroma and flavour that is a hallmark of North Indian restaurant cuisine. In Singapore, garlic roti is commonly served as an accompaniment to curries, dal, or butter chicken, and is a staple offering at Indian tandoor restaurants such as Mr Tandoor. Nutritionally, one piece (~90 g) provides approximately 286 kcal, 8.5 g protein, 9.2 g fat (of which ~4.1 g is saturated from butter), and 43.7 g carbohydrate with 3.4 g dietary fibre from the whole wheat base. The glycemic index is approximately 62 (medium GI), making it a moderate glycemic carbohydrate source. Clinically, individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should pair it with high-fibre accompaniments to moderate postprandial glucose rise; those with hypertension or cardiovascular disease should request minimal butter to reduce sodium and saturated fat intake. The whole wheat flour base contributes iron, B vitamins, and magnesium, while garlic provides trace allicin compounds associated with cardiovascular benefit.
common_user_queries: How many calories in garlic roti?
Is garlic roti suitable for diabetics?
What is the GI of garlic roti?
What is the difference between garlic roti and garlic naan?

confidence_reason: No exact ICMR-NIN entry for garlic roti exists; values are adapted from the ICMR-NIN chapati-with-fat entry with a documented butter adjustment. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database wheat chapati entry. Confidence is medium rather than high because the adaptation introduces estimation uncertainty.
notes: Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source database for this food category. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for Indian flatbreads. Vitamin B12 (vitamin_b12_mcg) is 0.0; this is confirmed zero as roti is a plant-based bread product containing no animal-derived ingredients except butter, and the butter quantity is insufficient to contribute measurable B12 at the per-100g level — confirmed zero per ICMR-NIN and USDA SR Legacy cross-check. Trans fat (trans_fat_g) is 0.1 g, reflecting trace naturally occurring trans fats from the dairy butter used in finishing; this is not an industrial trans fat. GI category is medium (GI 62, threshold 56–69 inclusive). GL per serving calculated at 90 g serving size: (62 × (48.5 × 0.90)) / 100 = 27.0. Serving size of 90 g is an estimate based on typical Indian restaurant roti portion weights; actual restaurant portion may vary. Source adaptation documented in nutrient_source.source_match_notes.
